celebration: GLUE: I — the first word about ME. Now a child can write and read their own name in a sentence.

songs.title: N-N-Nut
songs.lyrics:
[Intro — whispered]
N! N! N-n-n!
(nnnn… nnnn…)

[Hook]
N! N! N-n-n!
What begins with N?
N-n-nut! N-n-nut!
It's a nut! (a nut!)

[Verse 2]
N! N! N-n-n!
What begins with N?
N-n-net! N-n-net!
It's a net! (a net!)

[Verse 3]
N! N! N-n-n!
What begins with N?
N-n-nose! N-n-nose!
It's a nose! (a nose!)

[Verse 4]
N! N! N-n-n!
What begins with N?
N-n-nail! N-n-nail!
It's a nail! (a nail!)

[Bridge]
A potato?! (NO!)
P-p-potato! Not N!
(nnnn… nnnn…)

[Outro]
N-n-nut! N-n-nut!
It's a nut! (a nut!)
N! N! N-n-n!
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: The N sound is a hum through the nose /nnn/ with the tongue behind the top teeth — NEVER 'en' (letter name) and NEVER 'nuh' (schwa). The '(nnnn…)' lines are a nasal hum drone over the 808. Answer line 'It's a ___!' reuses the Week 1 frame.

songs.title: I Am an Ant
songs.lyrics:
[Intro — whispered]
(I am… I am… who am I…)

[Hook]
I am! I am!
Who am I?
I am an ant! (an ant!)
I am an ant!

[Verse 2]
I am! I am!
Who am I?
I am Sam! (it's Sam!)
I am Sam!

[Verse 3]
I am! I am!
Who am I?
I am a cat?! (a cat?!)
No, no — I am an ant!

[Bridge]
I can! I can!
I am an ant and I CAN!
(so small… so brave…)

[Outro]
I am an ant.
The ant is on the mat.
(shhh…)
Am I a POTATO?!
(NO!)
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: The whole song teaches the new glue word 'I'. One frame: 'I am ___.' — the ant boasts about who it is. In circle, swap 'an ant' for each real child's name ('I am Amy! I am Leo!') — the song is the machine, the class feeds it their own names. Kids choir answers the (…?!) echoes.

spreads.artNote: The Ant's entrance — a small shiny black ant standing upright with a proud little posture, spotlit tiny and dignified. Cast member joins.
The ant, chest out, declaring itself to the reader. First-person 'I' arrives — the child reads a word about a self.
The tiny ant seated dead-center on the huge woven mat, looking pleased with the whole arrangement.

spreads.text: The cat sat on the mat.
spreads.artNote: The tabby cat lands on the same mat, enormous next to the ant — tension.

spreads.text: The cat is on the ant!
spreads.artNote: SUSPENSE. The cat's paw and belly loom directly over the tiny ant — is the ant squashed? Dark, dramatic backlight.

spreads.text: I can! I am an ant!
spreads.artNote: The ant pops out unharmed and triumphant from under the cat, fist raised. 'I can!' — brave and unsquashable. The joke: the smallest is the toughest.
Sam (blue dungarees) and Segina (red dress) sit down on the mat beside the cat, joining the pile.

spreads.text: I am an ant. The ant is on the mat.
spreads.artNote: Group portrait — the tiny ant standing proud on the mat with Sam, Segina and the cat all gathered round, a whole cast now.
The potato photobombs beside the tiny ant, who looks deeply offended. The gag noun is left blank — the class SHOUTS 'POTATO' from the picture. Deadpan potato.

sentences: The ant sat on the mat.
A man sat on the mat.
extras: Heart-word card: I (taught as the word about ME — every child writes their own name after it: 'I am ___')
Moveable alphabet: build a-n, a-n-t, c-a-n, m-a-n, t-a-n
Sandpaper letter 'n' + sand tray — hum the nasal /n/
Name-swap circle game: 'I am an ant' → 'I am (child's name)'
Ant wooden figure added to the cast basket

teacherFocus: The big glue word lands this week: I. It is the first word about the child themselves — teach it as 'the word about ME' and every child writes their own name straight after it ('I am ___'), reading a true sentence about who they are. Meanwhile blend a-n, a-n-t, c-a-n, m-a-n, and meet the Ant: the tiniest, bravest member of the cast — small enough to sit under the cat and pop out saying 'I can!'. The name-swap circle game turns every child into the star.
